title_en: Scholarly Reception and the Tradition of Commentary
content_en:
Al-Furuq was recognized as a major scholarly achievement from its completion and has remained a foundational text in Maliki legal education and scholarship. Its most important early engagement came from Ibn al-Shatt (d. 723 AH), a Maliki jurist from Tlemcen in the Maghreb, who produced a detailed critical commentary titled Idrar al-Shuruq ala Anwa al-Furuq. Ibn al-Shatt's commentary is remarkable for its intellectual independence: rather than simply explaining or defending al-Qarafi's distinctions, he subjects them to rigorous critical examination, identifies cases where he believes al-Qarafi's analysis is mistaken, and proposes corrections. This critical dialogue was incorporated into the standard editions of Al-Furuq, giving readers access to both al-Qarafi's original argument and his most important critic in a single text.

The Ottoman period saw continued engagement with Al-Furuq as a reference work for Maliki jurisprudence. In the North African and Egyptian scholarly tradition, knowledge of al-Qarafi's distinctions was considered part of the formation of a properly educated Maliki jurist. The work is cited extensively in later Maliki encyclopedias and commentaries, particularly in the discussions of commercial transactions and procedure where al-Qarafi's analysis was felt to be most incisive.

Modern scholarship has found Al-Furuq exceptionally rich for several reasons. Contemporary scholars of Islamic law have recognized al-Qarafi's work as an important early contribution to the theory of legal principles (al-qawa'id al-fiqhiyyah), a genre that has attracted enormous interest in the context of Islamic legal codification and reform. His treatment of fatwa versus qada' has been extensively discussed in scholarship on Islamic courts, legal pluralism, and the role of the mufti in Muslim societies. His observations about custom have been cited in support of theories of contextual legal reasoning.

The Egyptian jurist Muhammad Ahmad Siraj produced a valuable modern study of al-Qarafi's legal methodology that situates Al-Furuq within his broader intellectual project. The work has also been discussed extensively in Western academic scholarship on Islamic law, appearing in studies by Wael Hallaq, Sherman Jackson, and others who have found in al-Qarafi a sophisticated and analytically rigorous interlocutor for comparative legal theory.
